rooms.name: Reception
rooms.subtitle: The Entry Point
rooms.description: Where privacy begins. The first touch. The moment someone decides to trust you.

patientStory.title: Elena's Story
patientStory.text: Because you ensured that sign-in sheet was private, Elena felt safe seeking treatment for addiction. Her employer in the waiting room never knew why she was there. She got help. She's recovering.

educationalItems.title: Patient Rights 101
educationalItems.fact: Under HIPAA, patients aren't just bodies in beds—they have actual power. The Big 5 Patient Rights: (1) Right to Access Their Records within 30 days, (2) Right to Request Amendments to incorrect info, (3) Right to an Accounting of Disclosures for the last 6 years, (4) Right to Request Restrictions on who sees their info, (5) Right to Confidential Communications. Bottom line: Patients own their health data. We're just the janitors managing it.

rooms.name: Emergency Room
rooms.subtitle: The Edge Cases
rooms.description: Where rules meet urgency. The hardest judgment calls happen here.

patientStory.title: Marcus's Story
patientStory.text: Because you held the line when that officer demanded records without a warrant, Marcus's mental health crisis stayed between him and his care team. He got help. He's still here.

educationalItems.title: When You CAN Share PHI
educationalItems.fact: HIPAA isn't trying to STOP healthcare—it just wants you to be thoughtful. You can share PHI without patient authorization for: Treatment, Payment, Operations (TPO - The Holy Trinity), Required by Law (court orders, mandatory reporting), Public Health (disease outbreaks), Law Enforcement (with a warrant), Deceased Persons (coroners, funeral directors), and Emergencies. The Catch: Even when permitted, still use MINIMUM NECESSARY.

rooms.name: Laboratory
rooms.subtitle: The Source
rooms.description: Where data is born. Test results, diagnoses, the raw material of PHI.

patientStory.title: David's Story
patientStory.text: Because you questioned that researcher's access request, David's genetic test results stayed confidential. He told his family on his own terms, not theirs. His story. His timing.

educationalItems.title: The 18 PHI Identifiers
educationalItems.fact: Welcome to healthcare, where Protected Health Information (PHI) is more valuable than Bitcoin and twice as regulated. PHI = The Big 18 Identifiers + Health Info. Think: names, addresses, SSNs, medical records, dates of birth, phone numbers, photos, basically anything that could identify a patient AND their health status. The Golden Rule: If you can connect health data to a specific person, congrats—it's PHI. Treat it like it's your own embarrassing medical history.

rooms.name: Medical Records
rooms.subtitle: The Archive
rooms.description: The long memory. Everything lives here. Access, amendments, the right to your own story.

patientStory.title: Mrs. Chen's Story
patientStory.text: Because you helped Mrs. Chen correct that error in her record, she got the insurance coverage she needed. A wrong diagnosis almost followed her forever. You stopped it.

educationalItems.title: Minimum Necessary Rule
educationalItems.fact: Healthcare is gossipy, but HIPAA has opinions about your oversharing habits. The Minimum Necessary Rule: Only access, use, or disclose the LEAST amount of PHI needed to get the job done. Not the most interesting amount. Not the juiciest amount. The MINIMUM. The Test: Ask yourself 'Do I NEED this to do my actual job, or do I just WANT to know?' If it's want? Close the chart. Walk away. Get a hobby.

rooms.name: IT Office
rooms.subtitle: The Vault
rooms.description: The invisible infrastructure. Encryption, access controls, breach detection.

patientStory.title: 50,000 Patients Protected
patientStory.text: Because you caught that phishing attempt, 50,000 patient records stayed secure. Fifty thousand people who will never know how close they came. That's the point.

educationalItems.title: Technical Safeguards
educationalItems.fact: HIPAA's Security Rule demands three types of safeguards: Administrative (policies, training, risk assessments), Physical (locks, badge access, workstation security), and Technical (encryption, access controls, audit logs). The Three A's: Authentication (prove who you are), Authorization (prove you should have access), and Audit (prove what you did). Strong passwords, automatic logoffs, and encryption aren't optional—they're the walls that keep the vault secure.

rooms.name: Break Room
rooms.subtitle: The Human Factor
rooms.description: Where people are just people. Gossip, venting, casual lapses. The hardest place to stay vigilant.

patientStory.title: James's Story
patientStory.text: Because you changed the subject when your coworkers started gossiping, James's coworker—sitting two tables away—never learned about his cancer diagnosis. He told people when he was ready. His news. His way.

educationalItems.title: The Art of the HIPAA Shutdown
educationalItems.fact: Not everyone who asks for PHI gets it. Sometimes your job is to be the bouncer at Club Medical Records. Automatic NO Situations: Family Members Without Authorization, Employers asking for diagnosis (they get work notes only), Marketing Without Authorization, Anyone Via Unsecured Communication (no texting lab results!). The Script: 'I'm unable to confirm or deny any patient information without proper authorization.' Saying no to inappropriate requests is literally your job.
